clipping-mask.definition: A layer that only shows up where the layer below it has pixels. Right-click → Create Clipping Mask, or Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + G. Keeps your lighting layers stuck to the subject.

multiply.definition: Blend mode. Darkens. White becomes invisible, black stays black. Use for shadows.

screen.definition: Blend mode. Brightens. Black becomes invisible, white stays white. Use for highlights, rim light, glow.

overlay-soft-light.definition: Blend modes that boost contrast and let color "tint" the layer below. Use for color spill and grade. Soft Light is the gentler version.

linear-dodge.definition: Blend mode. Like Screen but stronger. Use for the brightest hot spots.

stamp-visible.definition: <kbd>Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + Alt + E</kbd>. Creates a flat copy of everything visible as a new layer. Use it before bloom and grain.

camera-raw-filter.definition: Filter → Camera Raw Filter. Run on a stamped layer for grain, vignette, and color grading sliders.

low-flow-brush.definition: A soft round brush with Flow set to 8–15% in the top toolbar. Builds light up gradually instead of all at once.

alt-click-sample.definition: With the Brush tool active, hold <kbd>Alt</kbd> and click any pixel to set that pixel's color as your brush color. Use this to pick rim and spill colors from the scene.

lut.definition: A preset color table. Apply via Layer → New Adjustment Layer → Color Lookup. Always drop opacity to 30–50%.

smart-object.definition: A wrapper layer that protects the original pixels. Right-click the layer → Convert to Smart Object. Filters applied to a Smart Object stay editable — double-click the filter name in the Layers panel to reopen and tweak — instead of being baked into the pixels.

s-curve.definition: A subtle S-shaped Curves adjustment on master RGB. Drop one anchor in the middle of the diagonal, then drag the lower portion down a touch and the upper portion up a touch. The line bends into a soft S — that's the name. Adds contrast without crushing detail.

vibrance.definition: A saturation control that boosts low-saturation pixels more than already-saturated ones, with built-in protection for skin tones. Use Vibrance when you want a global lift; use Hue/Saturation with a specific range when you want surgical control. Master saturation in Hue/Saturation is rarely the right tool.
